titleEn: O Slave, You Have Become a Tale
translationEn: O Abdun, you have become a tale that goes the rounds of all news. You have bestowed yourself upon the Christians openly, concealing none of their secrets. Through you, I have reached among the Muslims what was long overdue of their blood feud. I saw their cowardly warriors, who could not be subdued by the edge of razors and their repeated passing. I did not know before this that you loved the whips and their fruits!

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: a tale, story, anecdote (that circulates among people)
to record, to document, to put into writing
to bestow, to grant (a gift or favor)
blood revenge, vengeance (plural: ثارها)
cowards, those who fail in battle
razors, sharp blades used for shaving
whips, lashes (plural of سوط)

context: A short satirical poem directed at someone named Abdun, mocking him for his association with Christians and his cowardice. Abu Tammam accuses him of being a scandal among Muslims, implying he enjoys punishment — a characteristic insult in classical Arabic invective poetry.

titleEn: O Gazelle, Speak to the Gazelle-Eyed

verses.ajuz: ما كنت أول وارد لم يصدر

verses.sadr: يا ليت شعري ضل عقلك كله

translationEn: O female gazelle, say to the gazelle with the wide, dark eyes: you have harbored treachery that is not hidden from you. Go — I did not grieve over you, though perhaps a remnant of my heart endured separation that could not endure. O you who came and whose missteps dragged you in — you were not the first to come and not depart. The days have triumphed over you, after your resistance, as sorrows triumph over an unrequited lover. Would that I knew: has all your reason gone astray, or are these the days of piercing the jewel?

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: one with large, beautiful dark eyes (especially a gazelle)
treachery, betrayal
a remnant of life or spirit, the last breath of the heart
missteps, slips, blunders (plural of هفوة)
to triumph, to gain victory over someone
piercing the jewel — a metaphor for a rare, difficult achievement

context: A brief ghazal in which the poet addresses a gazelle-eyed beloved who has betrayed him. The poem balances apparent indifference with lingering affection, ending with a cryptic question about whether the beloved has lost his mind or whether times have changed for the piercing of gems — suggesting rare transformation.

titleEn: Cold and Miserable, You Are Excused

verses.sadr: يا خلقة قد أمال الدهر أشطرها
verses.ajuz: لم يكفها من عقاب الله تغيير !
إن لم تكن أخطأت فيك المقادير
فكيف لو قد علت تلك الأعاصير ؟

translationEn: Cold, miserable, and a flint-stone — you are excused; the lions of the thicket are not bred by swine! Far from it — you raced to noble aims, chasing them ahead, yet cheese-curd and pickled fish weighed you down! I am excused for insulting a man whose nature was rotten, who was notorious for baseness. O creation whom time has tilted in its halves — no change will save it from God's punishment! Ghaylan and his followers did not err in their judgment, unless fate itself erred concerning you. Has even the breeze of satire blunted your edge? What then if those whirlwinds were to rise? Look at them — may God spare us their affairs: hands like boulders, yet honor as fragile as glass vessels. A glory that crumbled until its solid structure became rubble fit to repair towers and houses. Evil courts, praise be to God, now dead — in them, nobility is alive, but only the belt-strips remain!

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: cold, frigid; also a type of cold-bodied bird; metaphor for callousness
lions of al-Shara, a famous place noted for its lions — a proverb for true nobility
a type of fresh cheese, symbol of low or peasant food
pickled fish brine, considered base food — lowly occupation or status
Ghaylan ibn Uqba (Dhū al-Rumma), a famous poet — allusion to poetic tradition
whirlwinds, cyclones — metaphor for devastating satire
belts or girdles worn by non-Muslims (dhimmis) as a mark of distinction — symbol of abasement

context: A lengthy invective poem mocking a person of lowly origin and ignoble character. Abu Tammam employs vivid imagery of predatory animals, food metaphors, and references to pre-Islamic poets to contrast genuine nobility with the target's baseness. The final image of the zunnār (belt worn by dhimmis) signals the utter social degradation of the target.

titleAr: نعم الفتى ابن الأعمش الغث الذفر
titleEn: What a Fine Youth, Son of Al-A'mash

verses.sadr: نعم الفتى ابن الأعمش الغث الذفر

translationEn: What a fine youth, son of al-A'mash — the emaciated and foul-smelling — were it not for his baldness, his madness, and his bad breath! When he bares his teeth, it is as if his teeth are seeds of a gourd, hollowed out and rotten. How beloved is your mother — a woman of mankind — and may you be rewarded well on account of the loincloths!

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: emaciated, thin, worthless
foul-smelling, having a bad odor
halitosis, bad breath
to bare the teeth, to grimace
gourd, pumpkin (used here as metaphor for rotten teeth)
hollowed out, rotten at the core
loincloths, undergarments — used in a coarse insult

context: A short, crude invective poem mocking Ibn al-A'mash for his physical repulsiveness — baldness, madness, bad breath, and rotting teeth. The poem uses the ironic praise formula (نعم الفتى) before cataloguing defects, a technique common in classical Arabic hija. The final verse is deliberately ambiguous in its lewd implication.

titleEn: I Knew When You Plucked You Would Be Arrogant

verses.sadr: إن كنت تطمع أن قلبي هائم
verses.ajuz: بك أو تؤمل أنني لك ذاكر

verses.sadr: فأنا الذي يعطى استه من حاجة

translationEn: I was certain when you began plucking that you would grow arrogant, and I knew when you started exchanging that you would hire yourself out! By day you are a scribe, and by night entire you are a merchant! If you hope that my heart wanders lost over you, or that I long for you and remember you — then I am one who gives his backside out of need, and your father is my pimp, and you are the poet!

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: to pluck (hair) — referring to hair removal as a signal of passive homosexuality in classical invective
to act arrogantly, to show false pride
to hire oneself out, to become a wage-earner (with derogatory connotation)
scribe, secretary (day job)
one who wanders distractedly in love, lovesick
my pimp, my procurer (a grave insult)

context: A short, sharp invective poem using sexual insult, a common feature of classical Arabic hija. The target is accused of transactional behavior by day (scribing) and night (commerce), with a final stanza deploying a vulgar climax. This type of crude invective, while shocking to modern sensibilities, was a recognized poetic genre in Abu Tammam's era.

titleEn: O Maqran, Son of Foreign Women

verses.sadr: إلى النار في غير حفظ الإله

translationEn: O Maqran, son of foreign women's daughters, and offspring of the Jews — the worst of mankind! You have become a spectacle among people; you now ride fine horses after the ox. You traded a bitter man for one of spirit, yet your whip leaves no mark on him. He drags silken garments, and an old man of his by the Blessed River cannot hide. So say to Maqran: why do you remain when your harvest has arrived? Sell the sword and get a sickle instead, and exchange your whip for a shovel — then depart. Toward the fire, without God's protection — may God drown you, O you who have sunk low!

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: non-Arab foreigners, particularly peasants of low origin (singular: علج)
fine, agile horses — symbol of high status
one of spirit, liveliness, vigor
fine silk garments (plural of خز)
sickle — a farming implement, symbol of low peasant work
shovel, spade — laborer's tool, further symbol of degradation
one who descends, sinks, or degrades himself

context: One of several poems in this batch targeting Maqran, a recurrent subject of Abu Tammam's satire. The poem attacks his foreign lineage, his newfound pretension to aristocratic trappings (horses, silk), and ends with an ironic command to return to peasant farming — the ultimate insult in a culture that prized noble lineage and martial identity.

titleEn: O Abd Allah, Rise and Sit With My Estrangement
translationEn: O Abd Allah, rise and sit — I estrange you from me; I have cast you from my mind and thoughts. I have emptied my ribs of your love, though it once adorned my heart and chest. The elder scribes die of thinness, while your sustenance at sixty still flows! Your hypocrisy in roughness tells about you — that you exploit my patient endurance. You have surpassed all the wage-workers of Baghdad together, and have secured the pinnacle of every boast: those are hired day by day, but you are hired month by month!

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: my estrangement, my abandonment of someone
adorned, embroidered — draped across (the heart)
elder scribes, senior secretaries in the Abbasid bureaucracy
thinness, emaciation; also jesting — here used in the sense of dying of poverty
your hypocrisy, your dissimulation
wage-earners, those who hire themselves out

context: A satirical poem directed at Abd Allah, likely a scribe or bureaucrat in Baghdad. Abu Tammam mocks his hypocrisy, his comfortable livelihood at an advanced age while other scribes starve, and his exploitation of the poet's patience. The final image contrasts daily laborers with Abd Allah's monthly contract — making him the ultimate mercenary.

titleEn: I Bowed My Head Among My Companions
translationEn: I bowed my head among my companions while we were among those who drank and those who sipped. I nearly — and I erred — was slain by the memory of you, between roses and myrtle. O Ka'b, most generous in giving gifts, and with a face more brazen than Abu Shas! We have never seen its like for a lost estate, earned through generosity and valor. Have you forgotten my discipline and my covenant with you, sworn upon eyes and head! This, by my life, O Abu Ja'far, is the reward of one who raised the sons of men.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: to bow the head, to lower the face in shame or grief
my companions, those sitting with me (plural of جليس)
roses and myrtle — symbols of a festive gathering, wine party
most brazen, most shameless (superlative of صفيق)
generosity, munificence
valor, bravery, martial courage

context: A poem mixing ghazal sentiment with invective, addressed to Abu Ja'far (Ka'b) who is simultaneously praised for generosity and mocked for shamelessness. The setting of roses and wine establishes the courtly context, while the poet's bowed head and near-death from remembered longing frame the emotional register. The poem ends as a complaint about ingratitude from one the poet helped raise.

titleEn: Maqran, O Branching-Headed One

verses.sadr: لا تقس قلبا وابك من لم يكن

translationEn: O Maqran, you of the branching head, do not think yourself free of worry and anxiety! Do not harden your heart — weep for one who was not hard upon the sorrowful and the lovesick. The basil of the young men has now become pledged to pits and graves. And say to her: O my wife, your loss has shattered me — nay, O wife of all mankind!

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: branching-headed, with disheveled hair — suggesting confusion or unkemptness
anxiety, obsessive thoughts, whispers of the soul
the sorrowful, the grief-stricken
the ardent lover, one who is consumed by passion
basil plant — a term of endearment meaning a beloved or a source of delight
graves (plural of رمس)

context: A brief elegy (ritha) in which the poet mourns the death of a woman, calling her the 'basil of the young men' — a cherished beloved. The poem begins by addressing Maqran (possibly the woman's husband) and urging him to weep, before ending with the poet's own grief-stricken address to the deceased.

titleEn: The Heart Has Sobered After Its Trance
translationEn: The heart has sobered after it once saw itself intoxicated. I am not one who turns a face toward a scratching, unseemly talk. I have within me patience as a judge over love — a judge who takes no bribe. How can love flow clear for you, O namesake of Ibn al-A'mash? O namesake of Ibn Samha, morning and evening!

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: to sober up, to become clear-headed after intoxication or love-madness
intoxicated, euphoric, drunk on love
that which scratches, wounds, or is unseemly in speech
one who takes a bribe — the judge of patience is incorruptible
to become pure, clear, untroubled
namesake, one who shares the same name

context: A short ghazal in the mujazza' al-khafif meter, combining love sobriety with light satire. The poet declares his heart has recovered from passion and that his patience is incorruptible. The final verses address the beloved as 'namesake of Ibn al-A'mash' — linking the ghazal to the recurring satirical target of this batch.

titleEn: You Changed From Familiarity to Estrangement

verses.ajuz: وأعرت سمعك من يبلغ أو يشي

verses.sadr: لا مت إن كان الذي بلغته
verses.ajuz: حتى أرى في صورة ابن الأعمش !

translationEn: You have exchanged familiarity for wildness, and lent your ear to one who carries tales or informs against me. You claimed I am absent-minded — but who is it that is called the successor of Urwa and Muraqquish? May I not die if what I have heard comes true — until I see myself in the likeness of Ibn al-A'mash!

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: familiarity, intimacy, being at ease with someone
wildness, estrangement, turning cold and aloof
to inform, to tell tales, to slander
absent-minded, distracted, heedless
Urwa ibn Hizam, a famous pre-Islamic love poet who died of unrequited passion
al-Muraqquish al-Akbar, a pre-Islamic poet known for passionate love poetry

context: A brief ghazal lamenting the beloved's sudden change from intimacy to coldness, caused by a slanderer. The poet defends himself by claiming the mantle of the great pre-Islamic love poets Urwa and Muraqquish, and swears he would rather die than become like the despised Ibn al-A'mash — linking love poetry with the satirical target of the surrounding poems.

titleEn: By God, the Afflicted Son of Al-A'mash

verses.sadr: لو فر شيء قط من شكله

translationEn: By God, Ibn al-A'mash is afflicted in his posterior with pure wickedness! If the poor wretch could do what he is driven to, he would insert himself sideways. You are one who possesses many times the hatred that Qarun ever contained. You will surely know that total ruin is decreed for whoever grazes upon my honor. If anything ever fled from its own kind, then part of you would flee from another part. The fact that you were in the loins of our father Adam means we descended together to the earth!

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: the afflicted one, one tested by God with a hardship
wickedness, vileness, moral corruption
Qarun (Korah) — the Quranic figure of legendary wealth and arrogance, here used for legendary hatred
ruin, destruction, perdition
one who grazes freely, roams without restraint — here one who attacks the poet's honor
my honor, my reputation

context: A crude invective poem against Ibn al-A'mash containing explicit sexual insults common in classical hija. The poet threatens total destruction for anyone who attacks his honor, and uses the Quranic figure of Qarun as a hyperbolic comparison for hatred. The final verse — that both poet and target descended from Adam — is a bitter backhanded acknowledgment of shared humanity.

titleEn: Uthman, Do Not Busy Yourself With Muhammad's Mention
translationEn: O Uthman, do not busy yourself by mentioning Muhammad — the length and breadth of your own glory forbid you from it. His restraint consumes all of that, and his closed fist surpasses your open hand in noble deeds. It is as if your honor, in its smoothness, is his face — and as if your face, in its roughness, is his honor.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: to be preoccupied with, to repeat constantly, to harp on
to ambush and kill; here to consume, undermine, or secretly destroy
his withholding, his restraint (from giving)
open-handedness, generosity
smoothness, ease, gentleness
roughness, ruggedness, hardness

context: A concise madih addressed to Uthman, warning him not to compare himself to Muhammad (presumably another patron or man of note). The final verse is a master stroke of paradox: Uthman's honor is smooth like Muhammad's face (a compliment to Uthman), while Uthman's rough face mirrors Muhammad's honor — a subtle double-edged compliment showing Abu Tammam's rhetorical sophistication.

titleEn: O One Whom God Has Turned Away
translationEn: O you from whom God has turned away the world out of hatred for you! And O you whose very parts testify with hatred against his other parts! And O most burdensome of all God's creatures walking upon His earth — one whom even the King of Death found repulsive and was disgusted to take in his grasp!

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: to turn away, to avert one's face from someone (in disgust or rejection)
hatred, intense loathing
the heaviest, most burdensome — superlative of ثقيل
to find distasteful, to be repulsed by, to refuse
the King of Death — a poetic epithet for the Angel of Death
to find filthy, to be disgusted by

context: A short hija poem in the hazaj meter, targeting an unnamed person with extreme invective — so loathsome that God has turned the world against him, his own body parts testify against each other, he is the heaviest burden on earth, and even the Angel of Death refuses to take his soul. The poem uses religious hyperbole to achieve maximum satirical effect.

titleEn: O Amr, Tell the Rising Moon

verses.sadr: يا فتنة الناظر قد صرت في
ما كان في المخدع من أمركم

translationEn: O Amr, tell the rising moon: the tear has grown too wide for the mender to fix! O trial of the eye, you have become in your action the trial of the ear as well. Are you anything but a fawn that has betrayed its refuge, settling in the dwelling of a starving lion? Whatever was in the private chamber of your affair is now in the grand mosque! O how long I think of you — of one who carries a letter with a broken seal!

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: the rising moon — a classic term of endearment for a beautiful beloved
a tear, a rip in fabric — metaphor for an irreparable breach
the mender, one who patches tears — here unable to fix the damage
trial, temptation, enchantment
a young gazelle fawn — symbol of a beautiful, delicate youth
one who betrays, who abandons support at a critical moment
the private chamber, the inner room — symbol of secrecy

context: A ghazal addressed through an intermediary (Amr) to a beautiful moon-faced beloved. The poem laments that the beloved's behavior has made their affair public knowledge — what was once secret (in the inner chamber) is now known in the grand mosque. The final image of a letter with a broken seal seals the theme of violated secrecy and betrayed trust.

titleEn: O Utba, If the Nights Grow Long
translationEn: O Utba, if the nights grow long upon you — my poetry is an hour's poison! White hair visited you only bringing the morals of baseness and lowliness. I testify: you never dared against me but that Zayd al-Khayl was your slave in valor! And your face — when you contentedly offered it as a companion, you are truly unique in contentment! If you replaced it with another face, I could not face it by day in a crowd. Yet with it you have been granted a weapon — had you resisted, you could not have rendered obedience. Blood ties to dogs you have claimed — leave them, for they are not like the common tie of your own lineage. Relax your shoulders, for they were crushed to rubble by your jostling in the tribe of Quda'a. Do not be deceived by rabble who howled to support you with baldness and stitched rags. They saw me as their enemy and you as their partner in the trade!

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: an hour's poison — metaphor for a deadly swift blow
white hair, old age — its arrival usually brings wisdom, here brings only vice
Zayd al-Khayl, a famous warrior of the early Islamic period — used as a byword for courage
unique, one of a kind (literally: a weave unto yourself) — here used ironically
contentment, satisfaction with what one has (here: satisfaction with an ugly face)
the tribe of Quda'a — an Arab tribal confederation, here used contemptuously
stupidity, foolishness (also patching rags) — a double insult

context: A sustained invective poem (ten verses) against Utba, attacking him for his ignoble character, his dependence on the ugliness of his own face as a 'weapon,' his false tribal claims to the Kalb and Quda'a tribes, and his alliance with fools. Abu Tammam's invective here is socially sophisticated, attacking lineage, appearance, character, and associates simultaneously.

titleEn: I Shall Satirize the Lowly Maqran
translationEn: I shall satirize the lowly wretch Maqran — no wonder, and nothing new! A youth whose rear has never been rid of a crawling serpent. When it (the serpent) hungers, it grazes within his very person. What is inserted like an unripe date comes out like a wax candle. And I meet him with a slap that blinds eyes and deafens ears. For if he does not quickly understand the poetry, he will understand the slap!

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: a lowly, despicable wretch, a base person
a serpent that crawls — a crude sexual metaphor common in classical hija
the glans — used in crude invective
unripe date — used here as a metaphor in the explicit insult
a wax candle — completing the explicit metaphor
a slap on the back of the neck — the ultimate public humiliation

context: Another invective poem against Maqran in the hazaj meter. The poem is deliberately obscene in the classical hija tradition. It ends with a threat of physical slapping (the ultimate public degradation in Arab culture) as an alternative to poetic comprehension — suggesting the target is too stupid even to understand insults.

titleEn: She Extended Slender Fingers Toward Me
translationEn: She extended toward me a slender finger — smooth as a lizard's belly — describing separation, with an eye like a flowing spring. Her very words, in their knowledge of parting, nearly became tears from the tenderness of her complaint. But a blaming voice came upon me in the night's weakness — it would have been just, by your life, had it blamed one who listens! Should I blame one whose hands were stingy, who became soil for miserliness? How wretched that deed! I refuse and disobey the blamers, and go forth with my inherited wealth obedient to those who ask. Clothed in the garment of noble deeds — for these are shields made for the honor of the generous. And a veiled one I sought, and found him a star — distant to the weary travelers who seek gifts. When I lost his giving, I deprived him of my gratitude — and so we departed, both of us impoverished!

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: a slender finger, a fingertip
smooth and slender like a lizard's belly (أسروع = a type of smooth worm or lizard)
a spring, a flowing source of water — an eye that wells with tears
the blaming woman, one who reproaches the lover for his passion
my inherited wealth, my ancestral property
distant, far away — a star high and remote from those who need it

context: A poem that opens as ghazal — with a woman's tearful farewell — but pivots to the poet's declaration of generosity against misers. The poem defends giving and condemns the stingy patron, ending with the poet's witty declaration that when the patron withheld gifts, the poet withheld his gratitude — leaving both equally impoverished. This social critique is characteristic of Abu Tammam's praise-complaint poetry.

titleEn: Was He Not the Basil of the Describer
translationEn: Was he not the basil of the one who describes him, both for the one who seeks refinement and for the one who approaches anew? Innocent and naive — finding companionship in his states when he was like a frightened fawn. He sleeps at midday out of innocence and out of modesty, fearing the one who roams. But while your light was being guarded by your own modesty, you came with the sweeping flood. You were transformed — you who were the soaring, proud one — into the likeness of a bitch in estrus.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: basil — a term of endearment, symbol of fragrance and delight
one who seeks refinement and charm
innocent, naive, inexperienced (especially in love)
a young gazelle fawn — the classic image of a beautiful, timid youth
modesty, shyness, bashfulness
a sweeping flood that carries everything away; also a destructive force
a bitch in heat — the ultimate insult in the final line

context: A ghazal that narrates the transformation of a beautiful, innocent youth into someone morally corrupt. The poem begins with tender nostalgia for the beloved's innocence — compared to a frightened fawn and a basil plant — before brutally ending with the image of a bitch in heat. This trajectory from innocence to degradation is a recurring motif in Abu Tammam's poetry.

titleEn: The Abode Speaks Yet Does Not Speak

verses.sadr: يا سهم كيف يفيق من سكر الهوى
ما زال مشتمل الفؤاد على أسى

verses.ajuz: وضحت فكم من جوهر لا ينفق !

verses.sadr: إن العزاء وإن فتى حرم الغنى
همم الفتى في الأرض أغصان الغنى
يا عتبة ابن أبي عصيم دعوة

verses.ajuz: ما غبت عن بصري ظللت تشدق ؟ !

verses.sadr: وكذا اللئيم يقول إن نأت النوى
أو مثل راعي السوء أتلف ضأنه
ما زال في جرم بن عمرو منهم
قل ما بدا لك يا ابن ترنا فالصدا
أفعشت حتى عبتهم قل لي متى
verses.ajuz: فرزنت سرعة ما أرى يا بيدق
من بطشهم ما كل رؤيا تصدق

verses.sadr: سر أين شئت من البلاد فإن لي

translationEn: The abode speaks yet does not speak through its ruins of the truth that all new things will wear out. Abandoned ruins where the caravan gathered at its quarter, and where scattered clouds dispersed. My eye's corners welled with tears until I thought it was my very soul that was trembling. O Sahm, how can one recover from the intoxication of love — one who is parched, who drinks of separation morning and evening? The heart remains wrapped in sorrow, and separation remains wrapped around the one who loves. The nights have judged for themselves that they forever separate us and are never themselves separated. By my life, time has given sincere counsel — and among the wonders: a counselor without compassion! If you ignore the lesson of events after they have become clear, how many a jewel goes unspent! Patience — even for a youth deprived of wealth — is abundant provision for one who has no provision. The aspirations of a young man are branches of wealth planted in the earth, yet not every year do they blossom. O Utba son of Abu Usaym, a monstrous curse strikes your ears and stuns you! You fell silent when you saw me face to face, yet when I passed from your sight you began to prate! Such is the base man — he speaks when distance separates his enemy, and changes his tune in an instant when truth comes. A donkey saw the lion of the thicket and was terrified — but when it turned away, it began to bray! Or like the evil shepherd who lost his flock at night and woke up braying atop a mound! Far from it — you were deluded into thinking you could attain my glories, yet the reach is broad while your arm is short! You move from tribe to tribe — as if your mother or your father were mercury. Do your eyes squint toward the Banu Abd al-Karim? Woe to you — behind whom do you seek superiority? A people whom you see, when others are brought low, rise to meet great tribulations and then bow. A people who, when times grow dark, illuminate it — and time is left piebald from their light. Among Jurm ibn Amr there has always been an open door-key of generosity that never locks. No cloud of noble deeds was ever raised except from their hands it poured forth. Look — wherever you see swords gleaming, forever above their heads they flash and spark. Bold warriors — when the eagle of their banner flaps, the hearts of Death itself flutter in fear of them. And besides, when they put on iron armor, you would think they never imagined that death could be created. Say what appears to you, O son of Turna — for rust does not cling to refined gold. Have you lived long enough to criticize them? Tell me, when did you gain wisdom so quickly, O pawn? Mutilation to the nose of Tayy' if you could prevail — even if your soul were hung from the Simak star! I see that you have dreamed you are safe from their violence — but not every dream comes true. It is you whom they mean when they say: the wretched man is hanged by every rope. Go wherever you wish in the lands — for I have a wall of men entrenched around you. And a tribe whose very fear makes the crowned king abstain — as if the world itself were sealed shut upon him. And poems that journey toward you like nightmares of terror or sudden calamities. They make you spring up and sit you down in fear, bewildered, as if you were about to be hanged. From a poet at whose gate all speech stands waiting, and in whose protection eloquence hides. Syria has been refined by him, the Hijaz made smooth by him, and the East tempered by him.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: ruins, remnants, crumbling remains of a dwelling
dung-heaps, traces of abandoned campsites — the ruins of a beloved encampment
to well up with tears, to quiver (of the eyes)
mercury, quicksilver — a metaphor for a person of unstable, shifting allegiances
a pawn in chess — the lowest piece, used as an insult for insignificance
pure refined gold — the highest quality
the star Spica (al-Simak), one of the brightest stars — used hyperbolically

context: The longest poem in this batch (36 verses), combining a nasib (elegiac prelude) on ruins and separation with a blistering invective against Utba ibn Abi Usaym, followed by a magnificent madih of the Banu Abd al-Karim (specifically the Jurm ibn Amr clan). The poem transitions from intimate grief, through savage satire using fable-like animal comparisons (donkey and lion, evil shepherd), to elevated tribal panegyric. The closing verses celebrate Abu Tammam's own poetry as a force that has shaped Syria, the Hijaz, and the East.

titleEn: Against Me Advances Utba the Clinging Wretch

verses.ajuz: هيهات يطلب شأو من لا يلحق !

verses.sadr: كم خلق أير لم يكن لك ظالما
verses.ajuz: قد بات وهو بحلق جحرك يخفق !

verses.sadr: لو كنت تعلم يا مخنث طائلا
فلتعلمن حر أم من وإهاب من

verses.ajuz: من كان في شك بأنك تغرق

verses.sadr: دع معشري لا معشر لك إنني

translationEn: Against me advances Utba the clinging wretch — far from it! He seeks the pace of one who cannot be overtaken! How many a created member that did not wrong you has spent the night quivering at the ring of your burrow! If you had any worth, O effeminate one, you would know that in lampooning me you are a fool. You will surely come to know whose mother's honor, whose hide, whose old record, whose new account is torn apart! You plunged into my sea and its old depths swallowed you — was anyone in doubt that you would drown? By God, if you glued yourself with glue to a dog in Kalb, you would be certain you are stuck! Leave my clan alone — you have no clan — for I am behind them and before them a destroyer for you. How many times have our swords companioned their spears between armies on blood that quivers! What drove you to me is a wonder: a blind guide of guidance, and a mute who speaks! Speak on — you cannot harm me, and you are the offspring of whores: you lie, and I tell the truth.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: one who clings, who attaches himself shamelessly; the persistent wretch
the pace, the lead distance in a race — metaphor for the poet's unreachable superiority
an effeminate man, a man who adopts female manners — a grave insult in classical culture
worth, value, usefulness
to plunge deep into, to persevere recklessly in
a destroyer, one who brings ruin — also a place of perdition

context: A continuation of the satire against Utba, this time focusing entirely on crude invective. The poem moves from the image of Utba being swallowed by the sea of Abu Tammam's poetry, to explicit sexual insults, to tribal boasts, to a final declaration of the poet's truthfulness against Utba's lies. The image of blind guides and mute speakers conveys the absurdity of Utba's attempted satire.

titleEn: Had I Not Been Fed Full of Foolishness

verses.ajuz: ما كنت ممن أود يا حلقي

verses.sadr: إياك أرضى يا ابن البغي لقد

translationEn: Had I not been stuffed full of foolishness, I would not have been among those I favor — O my throat! I try to please you, O son of a whore — but after drawing close, you come with a strangling! I deserve on your account to have both my hands bound to my neck. You deliberately cause revulsion — and if you could, you would carry it out to the people on a platter! Like one who digs up graves yet will not approach their shade out of fear.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: satiated, stuffed full — here with foolishness
foolishness, stupidity
my throat — an idiomatic exclamation of self-blame
son of a whore (literally: son of transgression) — a standard insult in hija
the neck; here to come at someone's neck, to strangle
to dig up graves — a metaphor for one who violates what is sacred or sacrosanct

context: A brief self-mocking invective in the munsarih meter. The poet begins by blaming himself for his foolishness in associating with the target, then pivots to attack. The final image — one who digs up graves yet fears their shade — is a brilliant paradox suggesting a person who commits the greatest violations yet is a coward in the face of their consequences.

titleEn: O Crescent Moon Now Overcome by Darkness

verses.sadr: نال مني فيك التلاقي من الحر
لم أزل عالما بأن ليس خلق

translationEn: O crescent moon now overcome by the dark of the moon — where is that radiance and brilliance? Meeting you wounded me with a burning that separation itself could never inflict! Time has exchanged your garment of beauty until it wore it out from newness to tatters. I have always known that no sweet disposition endures forever without being tasted to the end! Patience and consolation have been dammed up against my blood, my sorrow, and my longing — so go, for you are my divorce. The face of union was not darkened by the brand of love until the lovers became cuckolds. We have claimed that consolation is a matter of fortune — since you have claimed that love is a matter of provision!

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: the crescent moon — a symbol for a beautiful youth at the height of attractiveness
the dark phase of the moon — used metaphorically for fading beauty or loss
radiance, brilliance, the glow of beauty
wearing out, fraying, becoming threadbare
consolation, forgetting, the fading of love's pain
divorce, repudiation — used here as a declaration of emotional severance
to become cuckolded, to be dishonored in love

context: A melancholic ghazal comparing the beloved to a crescent moon that has been swallowed by darkness. Abu Tammam develops the theme of beautiful things destined to fade, and declares his emotional divorce from the beloved. The final verse — that consolation is fate just as love is fate — is a philosophical conclusion typical of Abu Tammam's sophisticated lyric poetry.

titleEn: Leave the Wretched Son of Al-A'mash to Weep
translationEn: Leave the wretched Ibn al-A'mash to weep over a disease that has kept him in bondage! The yellowing of his face — from no illness — betrays the wretch and what he endures! How wretched his disease — and the disease has piled upon him ugliness and baldness. My eye was kohl-lined by the ugliness of his face until the pupil of my eye languishes in the lead. These faults — if distributed among beautiful women — would leave them with nothing as dowry but divorce. You are ugly and have exceeded ugliness, until it is as if you were created from separation itself.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: bonds, fetters — figurative imprisonment in disease
yellowness of the face — a sign of fear, disease, or moral corruption
to betray, to reveal a secret, to expose what is hidden
to pile up, to heap something upon someone completely
ugliness, disagreeableness, being repulsive in appearance or manner
beautiful women, especially those who are carefree and desirable

context: Another poem targeting Ibn al-A'mash, this time mocking his sickly pallor, ugliness, and baldness. The poem uses the image of kohl to suggest that merely seeing him has blackened the poet's eye. The devastating final verse — that he seems to have been created from separation itself — turns a ghazal trope (the pain of parting) into a metaphor for the target's fundamental repulsiveness.

titleEn: Woe to You, Submit to the One Creator

verses.sadr: ليس يغني إذا تتابع أمر الل

translationEn: Woe to you — submit to the One Creator, for in the throat is a leader toward baldness! When God's decree follows one after another, neither plucking nor a thin coat of dye is of any use. Your withholding from me reminded me of a book — O you of the finest morals! I will not call it the Book of Detached Letters, but rather a book of dowry. What free woman among people ever generously gave her dowry to a friend after divorce?!

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: the One Creator — an epithet of God emphasizing His uniqueness and creative power
the throat; also shaving (حلق) — a pun linking fate and baldness
baldness, the shaved state — a recurring insult in this batch
to pluck (hair) — a vain attempt to hide baldness
the Disconnected Letters (huruf al-muqatta'at) of the Quran — here subverted into a metaphor
a marriage dowry document — used wittily to demand compensation

context: A short satirical poem that begins with religious exhortation and ends with a witty demand for payment. The target's stinginess reminds the poet of a Quranic document — but rather than the sacred Disconnected Letters, the poet calls it a 'dowry book.' The final question (what free woman gives her dowry after divorce?) implies the poet has been treated like a divorced wife owed nothing.

titleAr: ماذا بدا لك إذ نقضت هواكا
titleEn: What Appeared to You When You Broke Your Love

verses.sadr: ماذا بدا لك إذ نقضت هواكا
إن كان ذا من غيرة قد أضرمت
فاحلف بأن سواي لم يظفر بها

translationEn: What appeared to you when you broke your love and swore you would not even smell my back? You accept wonders yet grow angry that I compared you in some matters to your brother! Like she who begrudged the return of a greeting yet gave away her thighs and haunches! If this is from jealousy that has lit your heart and inner self with rage — empty and consumed — then swear that no one besides me has gained what I gained from her, and upon me be a vow if I meet anyone besides you. But if you refuse, then you have openly refused reconciliation — know, may I be your ransom, that this stands for that.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: to break, unravel, or undo (a covenant, love, or agreement)
to smell your back of the neck — an Arabic expression for not following or pursuing someone
to compare, to set side by side for examination
to be stingy with, to begrudge, to withhold
jealousy, especially romantic jealousy
may I be your ransom — a term of endearment expressing submission

context: A tense ghazal in which the poet confronts a jealous beloved who broke their bond after discovering the poet compared him to his brother. The sardonic middle verse — about a woman who withheld a greeting but gave her body — rebukes the beloved's misplaced priorities. The poem ends with a formal exchange of vows and a thinly veiled ultimatum.

titleEn: Raging in Turbulence, Dissolute

verses.ajuz: ما إن يبالي أي وجه يسلك !
فهي التي إن مت قبلك تفتك
لك وهو يأخذ منك ما لا يترك !

translationEn: Raging in turbulence, dissolute — he does not care which path he treads! It is enough shame for you that your mind constantly weeps over you while your face laughs! Do not boast of overpowering the cups by drinking them — for they are the ones who, if you die before them, will overpower you. How many nights have you seized them, while a companion spent the night seizing from you what cannot be returned! I have held myself back from you because of the enormity of your crime — and so too, when judges are mentioned, they restrain themselves.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: one who rages, who is violent and turbulent
a turbulent flood, a deep immersion — here a state of reckless indulgence
dissolute, one who tears away all veils of shame
to boast of killing or overpowering — to brag about one's power over wine
a drinking companion, one who shares the wine cup
they restrained themselves, they refrained — the judges' restraint as a metaphor for the poet's

context: A short moral-satirical poem targeting a dissolute individual who indulges in wine without restraint. Abu Tammam delivers a paradox: the target's face laughs while his mind weeps for him — internal wisdom against external recklessness. The wine that he boasts of overpowering will ultimately outlast and overpower him. The closing comparison to judges restrained by a grave case is typical of Abu Tammam's epigrammatic style.

titleAr: رغم أنفي من أن ترى مهتوكا
titleEn: My Nose Is Rubbed in the Dust Seeing You Exposed

verses.sadr: رغم أنفي من أن ترى مهتوكا
verses.ajuz: أو أرى لي ما عشت فيك شريكا

verses.sadr: صرت مملوك كل من ترتجي فل
كنت ألحى مقران في الكشح حتى

translationEn: My nose is rubbed in the dust from seeing you exposed, or from seeing, as long as I live, a partner for me in you. You have become the slave of every one you hope in whose purse there is nothing — while before you were a king! What thing made you forget me after me? As if I am your father after your father. I used to blame Maqran for turning away — until the vicissitudes of time turned me away from you!

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: my nose is rubbed in the dust — an expression of humiliation, being forced to endure something
one whose veil of honor is torn, exposed, disgraced
a slave, one owned by another — here figuratively enslaved by dependence
a fals, the smallest copper coin — a purse containing nothing of value
a king, a sovereign — the former dignified state of the beloved
to blame, to rebuke, to reproach

context: A short ghazal of disillusionment: the poet witnesses a formerly proud beloved become enslaved by his desires and hopes in others. The comparison to Maqran — a recurrent figure of the batch — in the final verse ties this love poem into the satirical sequence. The poet blames circumstances (the vicissitudes of time) rather than himself for the ultimate estrangement.

titleEn: Cut My Ropes, For I Am Fed Up With You

verses.sadr: لا أشتهي أن تكون لي سكنا
قد نلت منك الذي بخلت به

translationEn: Cut my ropes, for I am fed up with you — and leave me wherever you wish from your hand. I do not desire you to be my dwelling — enough is what you were to me and I was to you! You are of many colors, shared among all — so seek a friend other than me who is likewise shared. I obtained from you what you were stingy with — and gained neither worth nor attainment. Go wherever you wish, freely — may the flood carry you wherever it flows. And die alive because of a beard that has grown upon you — you were a king before it came. When you see a youth upon whose cheek a hair has appeared, know that he is already destroyed!

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: to be fed up with, to be disgusted by, to have had enough
a dwelling, a home, a place of rest — here emotional refuge
of many colors, variable, fickle — one who cannot commit to one person
shared, held in common — not exclusively one person's
worth, value, advantage gained
a beard — its growth on a male youth signals the end of his androgynous beauty

context: A declaration of separation in the munsarih meter, addressed to a fickle beloved. The poem opens with a formal repudiation (cut my ropes) and proceeds through disgust at the beloved's inconsistency, the worthlessness of the relationship, and a final devastating observation: the growth of a beard has killed the youth's beauty and his appeal. This poem reflects the homoerotic convention of classical Arabic poetry, where the beardless youth is the object of desire and the beard signals his transformation into an ordinary man.

titleEn: O Muwis, How Did You Find My Snares Laid

verses.sadr: كم من لئم قد غزته قصائدي

verses.ajuz: من سائل يرجو الغنى من سائل !

verses.sadr: ما زلت أعلم أن بحرك ملحة

verses.ajuz: في المدح سود وجهه في الآجل !

translationEn: O Muwis, how did you find my snares laid? Is my cunning not above the cunning of the most cunning? I deployed my poems and my intercessions against you — and you denied me! How wretched the wage for the laborer! This is my reward for defiling my ambition with you in ignorance — and such is the reward of the ignorant. How many a base man have my poems gone out to raid, persistently attacking him — yet gaining no advantage! May the Merciful not lighten my burden from me, for I let my judgment graze in the meadows of futility! Eve never gave birth to a more foolish beard than the one who seeks wealth from a beggar! That is the man who counts the months and tallies them, in greed to produce a foal from a barren mare! Your stingy nature's fire-sticks bewildered you when I tried to strike sparks while climbing toward the heights of giving. I gained from your bounty the most of what is gained — in appearance — and the least of it in actuality. I always knew your sea was brackish, and was all the more certain when I stood on its shore. And such is the fate of whoever approaches the base with quick praise — he blackens his face in the hereafter!

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: my snares, traps — here the poet's poems laid as a trap for a patron
cunning, deception, strategic maneuvering
my intercessions, my means of petitioning — poetic appeals for patronage
the extremely stingy, the miserly (superlative of شح)
fire-sticks used to kindle a fire — here metaphor for the spark of generosity that never ignites
a barren mare that does not conceive — metaphor for a miser who produces nothing
brackish, salt-bitter (of a sea or well) — a sea that cannot quench thirst

context: A powerful complaint-poem addressed to Muwis, a stingy patron who refused the poet's petitions. Abu Tammam simultaneously blames himself (for having sought wealth from a beggar) and lacerates the patron for his miserliness. The poem uses a series of brilliant proverb-like images: the fool who counts months hoping a barren mare will foal, the fire-sticks that produce no spark, the brackish sea. It closes with a moral warning: praising the base destroys one's own reputation in the end.
